title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita, 2008
GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot positions countries according to their GDP per capita on the horizontal axis and their government expenditure per primary student as a share of that GDP on the vertical axis. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000 in 2021 international dollars, while the vertical axis measures the percentage of GDP per capita from 0% to 40%. Points are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a wide dispersion of spending priorities relative to national wealth. Serbia sits at the highest point on the vertical scale, allocating over 40% of its GDP per capita to each student. Djibouti follows near 38%, with Albania and Ukraine positioned between 30% and 35%. In contrast, Kazakhstan appears at the bottom of the vertical range, spending less than 5% of its GDP per capita. On the horizontal axis, countries like Burundi, Niger, and the DR Congo cluster at the far left with GDP per capita below $2,000. Moving right, wealthier nations such as Austria, Australia, and Hong Kong appear in the $50,000 to $100,000 band. Within this high-income group, spending shares vary significantly; Austria and Malta spend over 20%, while Bermuda and Saint Kitts and Nevis spend under 10%. The metric is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices to allow for comparisons. The distribution shows that lower national income does not strictly predict lower relative spending shares, as several lower-income nations allocate a larger fraction of their GDP per student than many high-income counterparts.
